title: Joining Forces: The Spillover Effects of EPA Enforcement Actions and the Role of Socially Responsible Investors
abstract: We find that firms reduce toxic emissions at their local plants following EPA enforcement actions against nearby plants operated by peer firms that compete in the same product market. These reductions are more pronounced for plants located near socially responsible mutual funds (SRMFs) that hold these plants’ parent firms’ shares. Close proximity to SRMFs is associated with real investment in abatement measures to mitigate emissions. While plants increase emissions again in the long run, such reversals do not occur in plants located near SRMFs. Taken together, our results suggest that local SRMFs complement EPA enforcement in influencing plants’ emissions.

author.Sudipto Dasgupta*: Chinese University of Hong Kong, ABFER, CEPR, and ECGI, Hong Kong SAR, China
author.Thanh D Huynh: Cbus Super Fund, Monash Business School, Monash University, Australia
author.Ying Xia: Monash Business School, Monash University, Australia

article.1. Background, Related Literature, and C:
JEL G23 - Non-bank Financial Institutions; Financial Instruments; Institutional Investors G31 - Capital Budgeting; Fixed Investment and Inventory Studies; Capacity G38 - Government Policy and Regulation K32 - Environmental, Health, and Safety Law Q50 - General Issue Section: Article Editor: Holger Mueller Holger Mueller Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

Governments devote considerable resources to regulating the environmental impacts of firms’ production activities, and firms, in turn, make significant expenditures complying with these regulations. In the United States, the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) is the primary body responsible for setting up the regulatory framework for environmental monitoring under various statutes, such as the Clean Air Act. The EPA, however, is not the only actor in the area of environmental monitoring. Socially responsible investors, who focus on sustainability goals when making investment decisions, are arguably an influential stakeholder because they can use their voice and threat of exit to influence a firm’s environmental footprint (Dimson, Karakaş, and Li 2015;Dyck et al. 2019;Hong, Karoyli, and Scheinkman 2020;Krueger, Sautner, and Starks 2020).

The presence of socially responsible funds in a given region raises an important question on which evidence is still limited: do these investors complement the EPA in influencing environmental compliance by their investee firms? The answer to this question is not clear ex ante. On the one hand, some socially responsible investors may not pursue genuine social purposes, as suggested by recent studies (e.g.,Michaely, Ordonez-Calafi, and Rubio 2022) and thus, they may not exert real influence on portfolio firms to reduce emissions. On the other hand, EPA enforcement could alert socially responsible investors to regulatory risk to their portfolio firms, causing these investors to step up their oversight of investees’ emissions as these firms are more likely to come under the regulator’s scrutiny following an enforcement action. Whether socially responsible investors complement EPA enforcement is ultimately an empirical issue that we investigate in this study.1

We address this question using a unique setting in which the EPA first takes an enforcement action against a violating plant. We then investigate whether and how local socially responsible mutual funds (SRMFs) play a role in ensuring environmental compliance by their investees’ local plants that produce products and use technology similar to those of the violating plant but are not targeted by the EPA. We show evidence consistent with the hypothesis that, following a nearby EPA enforcement action against a peer plant, local SRMFs elevate their efforts to influence their investees to reduce emissions in both the short term and the long term.2

To develop our prediction, we introduce the notion ofcompliance slack, whereby a firm’s operating practice does not comply with environmental regulations, possibly unbeknown to the EPA, and is at risk of scrutiny, which potentially exposes it to an enforcement action. Compliance slack can arise because (a) compliance is costly and (b) given limited resources, the EPA is unable to detect and pursue all noncompliant firms (Yaeger 1991;Heyes and Rickman 1999;Greenstone, List, and Syverson 2012;Zou 2021;Xu and Kim 2022).3Similarly, the full extent of compliance slack also could be unknown to local SRMFs (possibly until they are alerted by an EPA enforcement action in a given region) because fund managers might not monitor investees’ plants on a regular basis.

With compliance slack, our first prediction is that a firm will reduce its toxic emissions at a local plant after observing an EPA enforcement action taken against a peer firm’s plant that is located in close proximity. We define peer firms as those that compete in the same product market as a focal firm based onHoberg and Phillips’s (2016) Text-based Network Industry Classification (TNIC3), which is calibrated to be as granular as the three-digit SIC. An EPA enforcement action in a given region could raise concerns among local plants run by peer firms that they could be the next targets of investigations.

The perception of heightened regulatory risk possibly stems from the EPA’s “neutral selection” inspection policy, which is in part based on geographic proximity to other facilities that are scheduled to be inspected (Shimshack 2014). Once the EPA uncovers a violation, the violating plant will be subjected to more intensive inspections (Blundell, Gowrisankaran, and Langer 2020), which increases the risk that nearby peer plants also will be visited and inspected by the regulatory authorities. Consistent with this notion, we analyze EPA inspections data and find that, once the EPA takes an enforcement action against a plant, TNIC3-peer plants that are located in close proximity are more likely to be inspected. These heightened regulatory activities in the area, coupled with the similarity in toxic releases among peer plants,4suggest that treated plants (i.e., the same-TNIC3 plants that are located in close proximity to the violating peer plant) would have stronger incentives to take actions to reduce emissions after observing an EPA enforcement action against a peer plant.

Consistent with the above prediction, we find that treated plants significantly reduce their toxic emissions in the 3 years following an enforcement action against a nearby peer plant. We obtain this finding using a stacked difference-in-differences (DiD) regression analysis while controlling for high-dimensional fixed effects. Given that our setting involves multiple events and plants become treated at different periods, the stacked DiD method alleviates concerns regarding biased estimates in the presence of treatment-effect heterogeneity raised in the traditional DiD setting (Gormley and Matsa 2011,2011;Goodman-Bacon 2021;Baker, Larcker, and Wang 2022).

The effect is economically meaningful. For example, following an enforcement action, the emission reduction by the treated plant is equivalent to a 22.6%drop in toxic releases measured based on the EPA’s Risk-Screening Environmental Indicators (RSEI) methodology (Schiller 2018). We check carefully for parallel trends to establish the validity of our stacked DiD methodology. In addition, our results remain robust when we proxy for plant-level emissions using an air quality index that is measured using monitoring stations located within a 1-mile radius of a given plant. We also find that the spillover effects are more pronounced when EPA enforcement penalties imposed on the local violating peer plant are more severe and when the enforcement action involves a violation of a major environmental law. These results suggest that a treated plant’s response is stronger when local regulatory risk is higher. Last, consistent with the existence of compliance slack, we find that treated plants’ responses are stronger if (a) the EPA had not taken any enforcement actions in the area over the past 5 years or (b) over the 3 years that precede an enforcement-action year, a plant’s average toxic emissions quantity is ranked in the first tercile among its industry peers.

The evidence of spillover effects is interesting in its own right because it addresses the question whether an enforcement action against one plant can encourage greater compliance among other plants in the same region.5As discussed above, in our context, an equally important question pertains to the role that local SRMFs play in influencing emissions reduction at treated plants. Specifically, we examine whether a treated plant’s reduction of toxic releases is greater when it is located near an SRMF that holds shares in the treated plant’s firm. Proximity is important for soft information-gathering even in the present day, especially in the fund management industry (Bai and Massa 2021;Lee 2022). We find little evidence that news of EPA enforcement actions is disseminated widely: while the EPA’s own news releases occur more frequently with respect to cases involving severe penalties, the media typically does not carry such news. Abnormal returns to the target firms, although negative, are not significant around the enforcement actions.6News of such enforcement actions is, however, more likely to reach local SRMFs (e.g., by word of mouth) than distant SRMFs. Proximity could enable a nearby SRMF to acquire more information about the investee firm’s local plant’s compliance and also facilitate monitoring via, for instance, more frequent visits or developing relationships with potential “whistle blowers.” Taken together, we posit that an enforcement action against a nearby peer plant that indicates heightened regulatory risk in the area could prompt local SRMFs to attempt to influence their local plants’ emissions-abatement activities via voice and/or the threat of exit.

Consistent with the above prediction, we find that, when an SRMF is located in close proximity (within 100 miles) to both an investee’s plant and the violating peer plant, the total reduction in toxic releases at the investee’s treated plant is twice as large as that in treated plants without a nearby SRMF. We also find that the effects of SRMFs decay monotonically as the distance to a treated plant increases.7

These results could be driven by self-selection bias, whereby SRMFs choose to invest in firms with favorable environmental profiles and these firms could cut emissions independently of the local SRMFs’ influence. However, we obtain similar findings by matching treated plants located near SRMFs with those located far away from SRMFs based on the environmental scores of the plants’ parent firms, suggesting that the self-reaction of firms that attain good environmental performance is unlikely to explain our results. Furthermore, a placebo analysis using emissions of local plants whose parent firms’ shares are held by non-socially-responsible mutual funds yields insignificant effects, suggesting that the role of local SRMFs is unlikely to be confounded by the presence of other local monitors.

We next provide evidence of both the “voice” and “exit” channels through which local SRMFs influence plants and firms. First, we contend that, following an enforcement action against a nearby peer, local SRMFs engage with local treated plants more intensively, possibly through more frequent plant visits (i.e., “voice”). To provide evidence indicating plant visits by SRMFs, we employ New York City (NYC) taxi trips record data from the NYC Taxi {&} Limousine Commission (TLC) for the 2009–2015 period. We identify taxi trips from mutual funds located in New York City to their portfolio firms’ local plants. We find that, following EPA enforcement actions against a local violating peer plant, there are more taxi trips from the SRMFs’ locations to their local treated plants’ locations.8In addition, we find anecdotal evidence from institutional investors’ websites that SRMFs also actively collaborate with other groups, such as nongovernmental organizations (NGOs) and external consultants, to monitor the environmental compliance of their portfolio firms.9

Next, we provide evidence suggesting that the threat of exit by SRMFs may affect how local plants respond to an enforcement action. We find that SRMFs reduce the portfolio weights of firms whose treated plants do not reduce emissions immediately after a local enforcement action (hereafter referred to as nonresponding treated plants), and this effect is larger if the SRMF is located in close proximity to the treated plant. In contrast, SRMFs increase portfolio weights in firms whose treated plants reduce emissions after observing an enforcement action against a local peer plant, and again, the effect is larger if the SRMF is located in close proximity to the treated plant. One interpretation of these results is that the business model adopted by socially responsible funds requires them to maintain portfolios of firms that achieve superior environmental performance. Accordingly, divesting from every portfolio firm that faces an increased regulatory risk is not a viable option, so that engagement with the treated plant (or the parent firm) to improve compliance following a nearby enforcement action could well be the preferred strategy. Consequently, the SRMF increases its portfolio weight in firms that respond, and exits those that do not, because these responses convey information about the firms’ capabilities for mitigating regulatory risk.10The fact that nearby SRMFs respond more aggressively to a plant’s responses again suggests their information advantage vis-à-vis distant SRMFs.

We next conduct a number of tests to examine (a) whether treated plants and parent firms respond, in the presence of local SRMFs, by adopting abatement measures in the short- and long-term; (b) whether the presence of local SRMFs affects long-term emissions reduction at treated plants and their parent firms; and (c) whether reducing emissions has any financial impact on the parent firms.

First, we study the adoption of abatement measures as an indication of plant-level actions undertaken to tackle emissions. For treated plants that are located near an SRMF, we find that, following a nearby enforcement action, these plants are more likely to implement abatement initiatives involving manufacturing process modifications, such as modifying equipment, changing input chemicals, or improving chemical reaction conditions.

Second, we examine whether treated firms (i.e., firms that operate treated plants near SRMFs) implement abatement measures at distant plants that are located more than 100 miles from the violating peer plant. We do not observe changes in abatement at these distant plants in the 3 years after a peer enforcement action. In the period running from 4 to 10 years after the enforcement action, however, we find that these distant plants are more likely to undertake abatement investments to reduce emissions. This gradual adoption is consistent with the nature of abatement investment, which is costly and takes several years to implement. It seems plausible that local SRMFs influence treated firms to implement abatement measures at local treated plants in the short run because these plants face an immediate regulatory risk; however, they may also promote a comprehensive plan to reduce emissions at distant plants in the longer run.

Third, we show that in the absence of a nearby SRMF, emission reductions at treated plants are not sustained in the longer term. Over the 4- to 10-year period after an enforcement action against a local peer plant, emissions at treated plants return to 100%of the short-run reduction amount. By contrast, treated plants that are located near an SRMF continue to maintain lower emissions in the longer term.

Fourth, we find evidence that the exit threat is an effective channel for disciplining local plants so as to discourage them from adopting measures that result only in transient reductions in emissions. As noted, SRMFs (especially nearby SRMFs) reduce their holdings in a parent firm when its local treated plant does not reduce emissions in response to a nearby enforcement action. The mere threat of an SRMF’s exit could cause the responding treated firms to keep their emissions low in the long run. We find consistent evidence that, when responding treated plants are located near a nonresponder, long-term reversals of emissions at these plants are mitigated. These long-run reversals are prevented completely when the local nonresponder is located in close proximity to an SRMF.

Fifth, we examine the firm-level emissions aggregated across all plants. Consistent with the plant-level results, we find that, while treated firms reduce aggregate emissions in the 3 years following an enforcement action, they increase their emissions again in the long run, but these long-run reversals are prevented in treated firms with treated plants located near an SRMF.

Finally, we provide evidence of the financial implications of compliance. We find that, although treated firms attain poorer performance (proxied by return on assets) in the short run when they reduce toxic releases and implement process-related abatement measures, they could benefit in the long run, as evidenced by improved performance.11
The question whether socially responsible investors “walk their talk” has been at the center of a contentious debate. On the one hand, a recent literature shows that socially responsible investors act on nonpecuniary motives when making investment decisions and that they are willing to sacrifice returns for social impact (Baker et al. 2018;Hartzmark and Sussman 2019;Barber, Morse, and Yasuda 2021). Socially responsible investors also engage with portfolio firms to improve their ESG (environmental, social, and governance) performance (Dyck et al. 2019;Kim et al. 2019;Dimson, Karakaş, and Li 2021;Naaraayanan, Sachdeva, and Sharma 2021).12On the other hand, several studies find that socially responsible investors do not influence their portfolio firms’ environmental performance and may even engage in greenwashing (Heath et al. 2022a;Liang, Sun, and Teo 2022). Socially responsible investors in the United States, in comparison with those from countries that maintain stronger social norms, are also found to deviate from their stated pro-E{&}S positions (Gibson Brandon et al. 2022).

We shed light on the above issue by studying a special role that SRMFs play in complementing EPA enforcement to ensure environmental compliance by manufacturing plants in a given region.13Examining the direct effects of SRMFs on a firm could be confounded by an EPA enforcement action taken against the firm itself, which usually forces the firm to reduce emissions. Our setting avoids this issue by taking advantage of chains that follow the EPA enforcement action, including the responses of plants that face heightened regulatory risk and those of local SRMFs, in the spirit of a comparative static exercise. Specifically, in our setting, an EPA enforcement action against a plant could alert local SRMFs that the local plants of their portfolio firms might not comply with environmental regulations and are likely to come under heightened scrutiny by the regulators. If SRMFs have neither genuine pro-environmental objectives nor are concerned about investing in firms with poor environmental performance, we expect that SRMFs would not influence local treated plants to reduce emissions following enforcement actions against nearby peer plants. We discuss the role of SRMFs and related endogeneity concerns in greater detail in Sections4and5.2.

Our study is related to a strand of the climate finance literature that examines the effects of regulations on the environmental behavior of firms. Prior research has examined how the response to environmental regulations is determined by corporate reputations (Karpoff, Lott, and Wehrly 2005), financial constraints (Cohn and Deryugina 2018;Goetz 2018;Bartram, Hou, and Kim 2022; Hou, and Kim 2022), supply chains (Schiller 2018;Dai, Liang, and Ng 2021), and corporate legal and listing status (Shive and Forster 2020;Akey and Appel 2021). Whereas prior research has focused on the direct effects of environmental regulations on firms, to the best of our knowledge our paper is the first to empirically examine the roles of SRMFs in complementing regulators.14We find strong evidence that local SRMFs complement EPA enforcement, especially in ensuring local plants’ long-run environmental compliance.
article.2. Data and Variable Construction:
Our sample includes plants operated by U.S. public nonfinancial firms over the 1990–2015 period. We will describe each data source below, andTable A1in the Appendix defines all the key variables.

2.1 Plant-level toxic releases measures

We obtain a plant’s toxic emissions information from the EPA’s Toxics Release Inventory (TRI) program. The EPA requires all plants that meet their reporting criteria (e.g., carrying at least 10 employees, operating in certain industry sectors, and using certain type of chemicals) to report emissions data.15On the reporting forms, plants are required to provide the number of pounds per chemical that are released into the ground, air, and water. The TRI database also provides information on the plant’s identifier, each chemical’s identification information, quantities of each chemical released on-site at the plant each year, and quantities of each chemical transferred off-site to other locations each year.

To identify whether a chemical in the TRI is harmful to humans, we obtain information on the toxicity of chemicals from the EPA’s Integrated Risk Information System (IRIS), which assesses, characterizes, and standardizes the health hazards of over 400 chemicals in the environment.16Using the common chemical identifier—Chemical Abstract Services (CAS) numbers—we match IRIS chemicals with TRI data to identify chemicals that can harm human health. Our plant-level toxic emissions measure,Toxic, is then a plant’s total quantity of on-site releases of all identified harmful chemicals. To ensure that toxic releases are comparable across plants, we standardize toxic releases using the industry mean and standard deviation in each year. We confirm that our results do not change qualitatively when we do not standardize toxic releases. In addition, we employ the Risk-Screening Environmental Indicators (RSEI) score, provided by the EPA, as an alternative measure of a plant’s toxic releases.RSEIis an aggregate weighted measure of chemical releases, incorporating the chemical’s fate and transport through the environment, each chemical’s relative toxicity, and potential for human exposure, among other factors.ln(RSEI)is the natural logarithm of one plus a plant’sRSEIscore.

Plants are mandated to disclose their toxic releases, meaning the TRI data could be subject to self-report bias. Recent research, however, suggests that such bias is idiosyncratic and on average likely to be negligible (Brehm and Hamilton 1996;EPA 1998;De Marchi and Hamilton 2006;Akey and Appel 2021). Moreover, truthful reports of high emissions are not automatically penalized (Greenstone 2003). Indeed, the EPA’s Incentives for Self-Policing: Discovery, Disclosure, Correction and Prevention of Violations policy is designed to encourage plants to disclose violations voluntarily, allowing violators to avoid up to 100%of severity-based penalties and criminal prosecution if they are cooperative and their disclosure meets the conditions specified under the policy.17In contrast, plants that misreport their emissions can face civil penalties or criminal charges (Greenstone 2003;Xu and Kim 2022).

Although high emission quantities are not automatically penalized, this does not necessarily imply that plants do not have to reduce emissions.Blundell, Gowrisankaran, and Langer (2020)document that, once the EPA uncovers a violation, it will place the violating plant under “violator” status, which subjects the plant to additional inspections, potentially uncovering additional violations and generating additional fines. Plants will return to compliance status only once those violations have been resolved. Thus, the cost to the plant of being a violator comes not only from the investment costs required to resolve outstanding violations but also from stricter regulatory oversight. Given these costs, plants have strong incentives to reduce toxic releases if they wish to avoid being labeled as violators. Moreover, SRMFs that are located in the area near a violating plant can also exert pressure on investees’ nearby plants to reduce toxic releases.

2.2 Plant-level data

We obtain information on plants operated by U.S. public firms between 1990 and 2015 from the National Establishment Time-Series (NETS) database, which is supplied by Dun and Bradstreet (D{&}B) and is maintained by Walls and Associates. An important feature of the NETS database is that it does not suffer from survivorship bias (Addoum, Ng, and Ortiz-Bobea 2020). Each plant is given a unique identifier (DUNS number), which does not change even when the plant changes its location. This thereby allows us to trace a plant’s locations throughout its entire life. We obtain the plant’s historical location information (FIPS code, state, county, and longitude and latitude coordinates) and its parent company names from NETS. We then match parent companies in the NETS database with firms in Compustat and the Center for Research in Security Prices (CRSP) by their historical legal names, supplemented with careful manual checking. We merge plant-level toxic emissions data from the TRI with historical plant locations from NETS using a TRI-linking table obtained from the EPA, which provides links between DUNS numbers and TRI plant identifiers. We manually match TRI plants that do not have DUNS numbers with plants in NETS using plant identifying information, such as location, name, and the parent firm’s name.

Neumark, Wall, and Zhang (2011)find that the correlations between NETS and Current Employment Statistics (CES) and Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages (QCEW) are 0.99 and 0.95 at the county-by-industry level, respectively.Barrot and Nanda (2020)note that business entities are required to register with Dun {&} Bradstreet (which supplies the source data to NETS) and obtain a DUNS number if they wish to bid on government contracts. On the other hand, while the NETS data are fairly reliable for cross-sectional analyses, they have been found to be problematic for tracking establishment- and firm-level outcomes over time and thus for analyzing business dynamism (see, e.g.,Crane and Decker 2020).

2.3 EPA enforcement actions data

We obtain data on all enforcement actions from the EPA’s Integrated Compliance Information System (ICIS), which contains civil, judicial, and administrative federal EPA enforcement cases. The ICIS provides historical records for all enforcement actions taken by the EPA under the following environmental statutes: the Clean Air Act (CAA), the Clean Water Act (CWA), the Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA), the Emergency Planning and Community Right-to-Know Act (EPCRA) Section 313, the Toxic Substances Control Act (TSCA), the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA), the Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act (CERCLA or Superfund), the Safe Drinking Water Act (SDWA), and the Marine Protection, Research, and Sanctuaries Act (MPRSA).

We collect all formal EPA enforcement cases that result in a conclusion (i.e., a final decision). For each enforcement case, we obtain information on the case-unique identifier, milestone dates (from initiation to conclusion), plants charged in the case, and the magnitudes of the penalties (i.e., state/local penalties, the total compliance costs, and federal cost recovery totals). We match these enforcement cases with NETS/TRI plants using the TRI-linking table provided by the EPA. We identify a plant as violator if the EPA takes an enforcement action against it in a given year.

2.4 Text-Based Network Industry Classifications and peer firms

To identify a firm’s competing peers in the same product market, we employHoberg and Phillips’s (2016) Text-based Network Industry Classifications (TNIC), which are based on firm-level pairwise similarity scores from a textual analysis of product descriptions in 10-K annual filings. Since our hypothesis concerns peer firms that operate in the same product market, TNIC is more suitable for our empirical analysis because it is time-varying and is updated as firms change their products. We use two types of classification: TNIC3, which is calibrated to approximate three-digit SIC codes, and TNIC2, which is constructed to match the coarseness level of two-digit SIC classifications (Hoberg and Phillips 2010,2010). Only firm pairs competing in the same TNIC3 product market are identified as competing peers, while firm pairs identified as competing in the same TNIC2 but different TNIC3 product markets are deemed nonpeers.

2.5 Sample and summary statistics

After combining TRI data, NETS data, enforcement actions data, and TNIC data for each plant, we identify its nearby TNIC3-peer plants that are located within a radius of 100 miles.18In a given year, we identify a plant as a treated plant if the EPA takes an enforcement action against any of its nearby (i.e., located within 100 miles) peer plants during the year. Since a treated plant’s own environmental violation can confound the effect of its peer’s violation, we remove plant-year observations if, in the event year and the 3 years following the enforcement action, the EPA takes an enforcement action against the treated plant itself. To construct a control group of plants whose products are reasonably related to those of the treated plant and the violating peer plant, we drop plants operated by firms that do not compete in the same TNIC2 product market. We also require plants to have toxic release information and location data available as well as to have nonmissing firm-level control variables.

After constructing cohorts of treated and control plants for the stacked difference-in-differences (DiD) framework, we obtain our final sample, which contains 158,211 plant-year observations between 1990 and 2015. An average firm in our sample has a market capitalization of $2.54 billion, aBook-to-marketratio of 0.49, aReturn on assetsratio of 0.14, sales of $3.23 billion, and aLeverageratio of 0.20.

article.3. Spillover Effects of EPA Enforcement:
Our analysis focuses on nontarget plants, defined as those that in a given year had not experienced an EPA enforcement action within the preceding 3 years or within the following 3 years. The first hypothesis we examine in this study is that, in the presence of compliance slack, nontarget plants respond to an EPA enforcement action against a nearby TNIC3-peer plant by reducing harmful toxic releases. To motivate the use of the Hoberg and Phillips TNIC3 classification, inInternetAppendixTable IA1, we examine whether plant pairs competing in the same TNIC3 product market release similar chemicals that are harmful to human health. Using a plant-pair-level measure of correlations of toxic releases between TNIC plant-pairs and a firm-pair-level measure of technological proximity between TNIC firm-pairs, we find that these correlations are stronger among TNIC3 peers than among those in the same TNIC2 industry (but not the same TNIC3 industry). These results suggest that TNIC3 reasonably reflects the similarity in harmful toxic releases as well as the similarity in technology between peer firms.

3.1 Nontarget plants’ responses to nearby enforcement actions

Having shown that peer plants in the same TNIC3 tend to release similar harmful chemicals (compared to nonpeer plants), we now examine whether nontarget plants reduce their toxic releases after the EPA takes an enforcement action against a peer plant located in close proximity. We use a stacked difference-in-differences (DiD) framework to compare changes in toxic releases by treated plants and control plants around EPA enforcement actions. It is useful to note that our stacked DiD approach differs from the traditional generalized DiD method. In our setting, where there are multiple periods and plants are treated at different points in time, estimation bias may arise with the generalized DiD method as the control group contains previously treated plants and treated plants may have already been treated (Goodman-Bacon 2021;Sun and Abraham 2021;Roth et al. 2022). To address these issues with the traditional DiD approach, we follow recent studies and use a stacked DiD method which removes all control plants that have been treated (Gormley and Matsa 2011,2011;Goodman-Bacon 2021;Baker, Larcker, and Wang 2022). Nevertheless, we also confirm that all of our results continue to hold when we use the generalized DiD approach, although the economic magnitudes of the estimated treatment effects are larger in the generalized DiD setting.

FollowingGormley and Matsa (2011), for each year in which enforcement actions occur, we construct a sample (cohort) of treated plants and control plants using plant-year observations for the 3 years before and the 3 years after an enforcement event, removing the event year. Treated plants are those that are not targeted by the EPA but are located within 100-mile radius of violating TNIC3-peer plants.19Control plants include (a) plants operated by the same parent firm as a treated plant but located outside the 100-miles radius of the violating peer plant, (b) plants operated by other TNIC3-peer firms located outside the 100-mile radius of the violating peer plant, and (c) plants operated by firms that compete outside the TNIC3 product market but in the same TNIC2 market as treated plants.20We remove plants run by firms that are outside the TNIC2 network of a treated plant’s parent firm. To avoid any contamination of our control group, we require that control plants in each cohort not have been treated in a past cohort and will not be treated in the 6 years following an enforcement event (Baker, Larcker, and Wang 2022). This procedure results in a total of 20 cohorts over our sample period, each representing a year in which enforcement actions occur.21

Table 1, panel B, reports the number of treated plants and the number of control plants in each event year (cohort). On average, a cohort contains 313 treated plants and 2,206 control plants. We stack these cohorts across all years and estimate the following plant-cohort-year panel regression:

Figure 1depicts the trends in the differences in toxic releases between treated and control plants over the [−3, 3] window of an enforcement action. Consistently, we observe that, in the pre-event years, the differences are stable and statistically insignificant, suggesting that there were no pre-trends. Following an enforcement action against a nearby peer plant, the differences between the two groups become negative and statistically significant.25

Insofar as emissions levels are self-reported, it is possible that treated plants under-report emissions in response to an enforcement action to avoid attracting the EPA’s attention. As noted above, though, plants that misreport their emissions can face civil penalties or criminal charges (Greenstone 2003;Xu and Kim 2022). Moreover, in our context, an enforcement action against a peer plant could indicate that the EPA’s attention has been shifted to the area in which the plant operates, heightening the risk of scrutiny for other plants that operate in close proximity. Thus, to the extent that compliance slack is widespread, these nearby plants could have stronger incentives to report correctly following a local enforcement action to avoid penalties for misreporting.

As an alternative estimate of pollution levels, we use the air quality index from the EPA’s Air Quality System (AQS) database.26For a given plant, we construct an annual measure of air quality (AQI), which is calculated as the average of daily AQI across all monitoring stations that are located within a one-mile radius of the plant over a year. A highAQIindicates a higher emissions level. We repeat regression (1) but replace the dependent variable with the average annualAQI. The results reported inInternetAppendixTable IA2suggest that air quality around a plant improves after the EPA takes an enforcement action against a nearby peer plant.

3.2 Severity of peer plants’ violations and treated plant’s response

If a nontarget plant is concerned about ex ante regulatory threats coming from an enforcement action against a nearby peer plant, we expect its response to be more pronounced when the peer’s violation is more severe. To examine this prediction, we conduct two tests using two alternative proxies for the severity of the peer plant’s violation. In the first test, we employ the total penalty imposed on nearby enforcement cases as a proxy for severity. We then create a dummy variable,HighCost, that is equal to one if the total penalty is above the sample median and zero otherwise. In the second test, we obtain data on the type of each violation from the EPA. We create a dummy variable,MajorViolation, that is equal to one if an enforcement action involves major violations, such as a violation of an environmental law, a violation of a chemical discharge permit, or illegal dumping of chemicals, and zero for other violation types.

The results reported inInternetAppendixTable IA3suggest that a treated plant’s reduction of toxic releases is more pronounced when the peer plant’s violation is more severe. These results are consistent with the notion that a more serious violation by the target plant is likely to invite more intensive monitoring of the plant by regulators, and hence the likelihood that nearby plants are inspected by regulators is higher. As a result, nearby plants further step up their compliance and emissions reductions when the violation is of a more serious nature.

3.3 Evidence of compliance slack

A plant’s response to a nearby peer EPA enforcement action may be weaker if its local peer plants experienced EPA enforcement in the past. By contrast, a plant that is located in an area where its local peers have not experienced a recent enforcement action may respond more strongly, possibly because compliance slack is higher. InInternet AppendixTable IA4, we create a dummy variableNonPastExpothat is equal to one if the treated plants have not been treated over the past 5 years and zero otherwise. We then repeat the baseline regression but add the interaction term betweenPost-ClosePeerEAandNonPastExpo. The regression results show that the coefficients forPost-ClosePeerEA×NonPastExpoare negative and significant. Consistent with the prediction of compliance slack, these results suggest that the spillover effects are more pronounced when the EPA was historically less active in a region27

In addition, we use the relative ranking of a plant’s pre-event toxic releases as a proxy for compliance slack. If a plant releases more toxic emissions than its peers, we expect its response to be more pronounced as the plant’s concern about being the next target of an enforcement action grows. We construct two dummy variables,HighToxicandHighRSEI, to measure a plant’s relative ranking in toxic releases andRSEIscores, respectively. Specifically,HighToxic(HighRSEI) is equal to one if, over the 3 years before an enforcement action, a plant’s average quantity of toxic releases (RSEI) is ranked in the first tercile among its industry peers, and zero otherwise. We report the estimation results inInternetAppendixTable IA5. We find that the coefficients for bothPost-ClosePeerEA×HighToxicandPost-ClosePeerEA×HighRSEIare negative and significant. Consistent with the prediction that compliance slack plays a key role, the results indicate that treated plants react more strongly when their toxic releases are greater than those of their peers in the pre-event years.
article.4. The Role of Local Socially Responsibl:
In this section, we examine the role of SRMFs in influencing investees’ plants to reduce toxic releases following a nearby EPA enforcement action. While we do not observe engagement by SRMFs directly, we argue that if monitoring is an objective, SRMFs would prefer to invest in firms located in close proximity because it is easier and less costly for them to engage with local firms. Consistent with this conjecture, as shown inInternetAppendixTable IA6, we find that SRMFs’ holdings of local firms (firms operating plants within 100 miles of the SRMFs’ locations) with superior environmental ratings are significantly larger than their holdings of distant firms with the same environmental profile. We do not, however, find a corresponding result for mutual funds that are not classified as socially responsible. These results support the notion that local SRMFs acquire superior local knowledge about local plants’ operating practices. Since the treated plant and the peer violator discharge similar toxic releases, a nearby EPA enforcement action could prompt these local SRMFs to exert pressure on the parent firm (whose shares they hold) to cut toxic releases at the local treated plant.

4.1 Local socially responsible mutual funds and plant-level toxic releases

Examining the role of SRMFs requires us to obtain data from three sources. First, we collect data on actively managed, open-ended U.S. equity mutual funds from the CRSP Survivor-Bias-free Mutual Fund database. Our study focuses on individual funds (not fund families) for which location information is available starting in 2000. FollowingAlok, Kumar, and Wermers (2020), we assume that the offices provided by the CRSP Mutual Fund database are primarily responsible for making investment decisions for individual funds within a fund family. Yet, measurement issues related to an individual fund’s location will bias the analysis against finding any effects of proximity between a mutual fund and an investee’s plant-level emissions (Barrot and Sauvagnat 2016).

Second, for each mutual fund in the CRSP data set, we obtain its stock holdings from the Thomson Reuters Mutual Fund Holdings database.28To ensure that fund managers have sufficient interest in a firm, we require that a fund hold at least 0.5%of the firm’s total number of shares outstanding. We ensure that our results remain robust when we vary this threshold from 0.1%to 1%or when we use portfolio weights to capture the importance of a stock to a fund.29Last, for each firm in a fund portfolio, we collect data on its environmental scores from the MSCI KLD ESG database, which provides evaluations of various aspects of environmental performance, such as the adoption of waste management and greater use of renewable energy (Engle et al. 2020). Following prior research, we compute the environmental score,EScore, for each firm in a given year as the difference between environmental strengths and concerns.30

We followHwang, Titman, and Wang (2022)andCao et al. (2022)to identify socially responsible financial institutions. In each quarter, we sort funds in our sample into two groups, based on the average environmental scores of firms in their portfolio holdings, where the top group contains institutions with high average portfolioEScoresand the bottom group consists of institutions with low average portfolioEScores. Financial institutions in the top group are deemed socially responsible.31Note that we determine the SRMF designation using a fund’s holdings as of the year before an EPA enforcement event. Thus, the identification of SRMFs is not affected by an investee’s ex post response to an enforcement action.

After combining the fund location information, the fund holdings of each parent firm, and the parent firm’s plant locations, we create a dummy variable,CloseSRMF, which is equal to one if there is an SRMF that holds shares in the parent firm to which a nontarget treated plant belongs, located within 100 miles of the treated plant and within 100 miles of the violating peer plant. We then reestimate regression (1) but include an additional interaction term betweenPost-ClosePeerEAandCloseSRMF. We report the estimation results in columns 1 and 2 ofTable 3, panel A. We observe a negative and significant coefficient forPost-ClosePeerEA×CloseSRMF, suggesting that treated plants with SRMFs are located in close proximity reduce toxic releases to a greater extent than other treated plants.

The local advantages of SRMFs imply that the impact of these funds depends on their proximity to an enforcement action (Coval and Moskowitz 2001). To examine this prediction, for each treated plant, we create two dummy variables,SRMF(100,200)andSRMF(>200)⁠, which equal one if the distance from both the treated plant and the violating peer plant to the nearest SRMF, which holds shares in the treated plant’s parent firm, is between 100 miles and 200 miles and greater than 200 miles, respectively. We then repeat the regressions associated with columns 1 and 2 but include the interaction term between each of these dummy variables andPost-ClosePeerEA. We present the estimation results in columns 3 and 4 ofTable 3panel A. We observe decaying effects of SRMFs as the distance increases. For example, in column 3, the coefficient forPost-ClosePeerEA×CloseSRMFis−0.29, which is statistically significant at the 1%level. The coefficient forPost-ClosePeerEA×SRMF(100,200)is−0.018, and the coefficient estimate forPost-ClosePeerEA×SRMF(>200)is−0.002, These results suggest that the influence of local SRMFs on treated plants is stronger than that of distant SRMFs.

4.1.1 Pre-event trends and local SRMFs

To examine the differential emissions between treated plants that operate in close proximity to SRMFs and other treated plants before enforcement actions, we interact each of the pre-event year dummy variables withCloseSRMF. As shown in panel B ofTable 3, we find that the coefficients for these interaction terms are insignificant, suggesting that, during the pre-event period, emissions between treated plants located in close proximity to an SRMF and other treated plants do not differ from each other. These results indicate that signals of compliance slack are not precise enough for SRMFs to incur monitoring costs when the regulatory threat is low; however, a nearby enforcement action elevates the regulatory risk. These insignificant pre-event trends are also consistent with findings documented in recent studies indicating that ownership by socially responsible institutions generally does not affect a firm’s environmental performance (Gibson Brandon et al. 2022;Heath et al. 2022a;Liang, Sun, and Teo 2022). A nearby enforcement action can however alert local SRMFs about compliance slack in the area, prompting them to influence local treated plants.

4.1.2 The “self-reaction” hypothesis

One might be concerned that firms held by SRMFs perform well environmentally and thus might choose to reduce emissions even in the absence of local SRMF influence. This is an issue particularly because our method for identifying SRMFs is based on fund holdings in firms that perform well environmentally. We call this the “self-reaction” hypothesis. This hypothesis predicts that toxic releases should be lower across all plants of a firm, not just at the plant located in close proximity to the violating peer plant.32

To rule out the self-reaction hypothesis, we reexamine the effects of SRMFs using two samples of firms that are matched based on environmental scores (EScore). To construct the first matched sample, for each treated plant that is located near an SRMF (i.e., within 100 miles), we find a matched treated plant that is located between 100 miles and 200 miles from its nearest SRMFs and whose parent firm has the sameEScore. We remove treated plants that cannot be matched. We repeat this matching procedure based on parent firms’EScoresto find a match between the matched treated plants located within 100 miles of an SRMF with treated plants located more than 200 miles from the nearest SRMFs. This matching procedure ensures that we compare toxic releases between treated plants owned by firms whose environment performance is comparable, as judged by an independent rating agency, that is, MSCI.33To construct the second alternative matched sample, in addition to the matching procedure for all the treated plants, we also find matched plants operated by control firms in the same TNIC2 network that have the sameEScoreas the treated firms. This matching procedure further ensures that we not only compare toxic releases among treated plants—whose parents perform the same environmentally—but also compare toxic releases by treated plants with those by control plants owned by firms that attain the same environmental performance.

We reestimate the regression associated withTable 3, panel A, using the matched samples and report the results inTable 3, panel C. Consistent with the results shown inTable 3, panel A, we observe that the effects of SRMFs on treated plants’ toxic emissions decay as the distance between SRMFs and treated plants increases. In particular, the coefficient forPost-ClosePeerEA×CloseSRMFremains negative and significant, suggesting that our results are unlikely to be explained by the self-reaction of firms with good environmental performance. The coefficients forPost-ClosePeerEA×SRMF(100,200)andPost-ClosePeerEA×SRMF(>200)⁠, however, are insignificant. In addition, our results remain robust when we employ the propensity score matching approach to ensure that treated plants with nearby SRMFs are similar to treated plants without nearby SRMFs and to control plants, in terms of firm-level characteristics. We report the results inInternet AppendixTable IA7.

4.1.3 Is emissions reduction driven by a decline in production?

To examine whether the observed decreases in toxic releases reflect changes in economic activity, we employ the production ratio measure provided by the EPA as a proxy for a plant’s economic activity (Akey and Appel 2021). The production ratio, measured at the plant-chemical-year level, is a ratio of reporting year output to prior year output in which a chemical is used in the production process. We reestimate the regressions associated withTables 2and3and replace the dependent variable with the production ratio measures. We report the results inInternetAppendixTable IA8. We find little evidence that treated plants’ production ratios change following nearby enforcement actions.

4.1.4 Is the role of local SRMFs confounded by other local monitors?

Another concern is that SRMFs tend to be located in metropolitan areas where plants are subject to greater scrutiny by local monitors, such as the media and regulators. To examine whether the effects of local SRMFs are confounded by other local monitors, we conduct a placebo analysis using emissions by plants whose parent firms’ shares are held by local non-SRMFs. If our results are driven by local monitors’ influence, we expect to observe these effects for plants that belong to local non-SRMFs’ investees. The estimation results reported inInternetAppendixTable IA9suggest that emissions at these non-SRMFs’ investees’ plants do not change following an enforcement action against a local peer plant. As such, the influence of local SRMFs is unlikely to be confounded by the scrutiny of other local monitors.34

4.1.5 Evidence on soft information transmission

The effect of a local SRMF on a treated plant’s emissions is consistent with the proposition that proximity enables the SRMF to gather information about the EPA’s enforcement actions as well as the treated plant’s level of compliance. To explore channels that might facilitate soft information transmission, we conduct two additional tests. In the first test, we examine whether close proximity to treated firms’ headquarters could facilitate the transmission of information. As seen inInternet AppendixTable IA10, we find that the impact of nearby SRMFs on treated plants’ emissions is stronger when close SRMFs are also located near the headquarters of treated plants, which could facilitate both information gathering and the ability of the SRMFs to influence the treated plant’s abatement policy.35In the second test, we study the role of SRMFs with long-term investment relationships with treated firms. We report the regression results in panel B of Table IA10. We find that a long-term investment relationship with the investee firm of a treated plant is associated with a stronger impact of the presence of a local SRMF on a treated plant’s emissions, suggesting that such a relationship facilitates or incentivizes information collection, enabling SRMFs to influence firms’ toxic releases.

4.2 EPA inspections and local SRMFs

One of the main premises of our arguments is that nearby treated plants face a higher risk of inspection following an enforcement action. We now provide evidence pertaining to this premise. We also show that the additional emissions reduction that we attribute to local SRMFs is not driven by the possibility that the EPA inspects treated plants located near an SRMF more frequently than other treated plants (recall that the emissions reductions of treated plants located near an SRMF are twice as large as those of treated plants that are not located near an SRMF).

We obtain data on inspections conducted by the EPA or local authorities from the EPA’s ICIS database. We then estimate the stacked DiD regression associated withTable 3, panel A, but replace the dependent variable with eitherln(Inspections)orInspect.ln(Inspections)is the natural logarithm of one plus the number of inspections of a plant in a given year.Inspectis a dummy variable that is equal to one if the EPA inspect the plant at least once in a given year. We report the estimation results inTable 4. We find that the coefficients forPost-ClosePeerEAare positive and statistically significant. This result indicates that, following a nearby enforcement action against a peer plant, treated plants indeed experience an increase in both the frequency of inspections and the likelihood of inspection by either the EPA or local authorities. The effect is also economically significant. For example, the coefficient forPost-ClosePeerEAin column 3 is 0.11, meaning that the EPA increases the likelihood of inspection of treated plants by 11%after an enforcement action against a nearby peer plant. This result supports our hypothesis that nearby treated plants face a higher risk of inspection following an enforcement action. The coefficient forPost-ClosePeerEA×CloseSRMFis however small and statistically nonsignificant, suggesting that there is no difference in inspection frequency or the likelihood of inspection between treated plants near an SRMF and other treated plants. As such, our findings reported inTable 3, panel A, are unlikely to be driven by more frequent inspections of treated plants that are located near an SRMF.

4.3 Mutual funds’ taxi trips to plants in New York City

Monitoring can occur in many ways, but plant visits are arguably one of the most effective ways. To provide evidence pertaining to SRMFs’ monitoring at the plant level, we employ New York City taxi trips data collected from the New York City Taxi {&} Limousine Commission (TLC) and examine whether there are more taxi trips from SRMFs’ locations to local treated plants’ locations following an enforcement action against a violating peer plant. We construct a sample of treated and control plants that are located in New York City and operate near mutual funds who hold these plants’ parent firms’ shares and are also located in New York City. The total number of plants (either treated or control) are distributed as follows: Bronx (5), Brooklyn (11), Queens (14), and Staten Island (1). We drop the five plants located in Manhattan, although our results remain consistent if we include these plants.

We obtain information on NYC taxi trips from TLC Trip Record Data for the 2009–2015 period. article.5. Abatement Activities and the Role of:
5.1 Pollution abatement and local SRMFs

In this section, we provide additional evidence pertaining to the role of local SRMFs in influencing local treated plants or their parent firms to implement real abatement measures following an EPA enforcement action in the region. We measure abatement activities at the plant level using data from the EPA’s Pollution Prevention (P2) database. As part of the TRI reporting process, plants are required to report any activities undertaken to limit the quantity of harmful chemicals released to the environment. FollowingAkey and Appel (2021), we classify these activities into two categories: improvements in production process and changes in operating practices. Process improvements entail a variety of activities, such as optimizing chemical reaction conditions, modifying equipment, and other process modifications. Good operating practices involve improving maintenance scheduling, recordkeeping, and quality control, among other activities.39

In the next section, we provide evidence consistent with the idea that the additional monitoring of the local plant by local SRMFs triggered by the enforcement action transmits firm-specific information about compliance to the local SRMF. When the parent firm is noncomplaint, the SRMF may push for abatement measures not only at the local plant but also at distant plants. Such measures materialize with a time lag.

5.2 Further discussion of the role of local SRMFs

Our results thus far suggest that SRMFs influence local treated plants by encouraging them to adopt abatement measures to mitigate emissions in the 3 years after a nearby peer enforcement action. To the extent that SRMFs are concerned about firm-level noncompliance and the environmental performance of portfolio firms—whether because they have sustainability motives or for other reasons—we expect them to influence the treated investee firms to undertake abatement investments not only at the local treated plant but also at all plants. Yet abatement activities, by their nature, are costly and take several years to implement, and it is likely that the treated firms would implement the investments in a staggered manner, starting with the local treated plant because it faces immediate regulatory risk.

Control plants are those that belong to other TNIC3-peer firms (which do not own any treated plants) or those that belong to firms competing outside the TNIC3 product market but in the same TNIC2 product market as the treated firms. We also require that control plants’ parent firms not operate treated plants that were subject to a past event and will not operate treated plants in the next 6 years. We construct a dummy variable,Post-TreatDistant, that is equal to one for treated distant plants in the 3 years after the EPA takes an enforcement action against a violating peer plant and zero otherwise. We report the estimation results inTable 8. We find that, in the 3 years after a nearby peer enforcement action, the presence of local SRMFs has insignificant effects on process-related abatement at treated firms’ distant plants (columns 1 and 2). We then examine the abatement activities at these distant plants during the period spanning [4, 10] years after an enforcement action. We construct cohorts of treated distant plants and control plants using plant-year observations for the 3 years before and the [4, 10] years after each event year. We remove the [0, 3] years after an EPA enforcement action for distant plants owned by treated firms.40We find that, during the [4, 10] years after an enforcement action, the presence of a nearby SRMF is associated with a greater likelihood of implementing abatement measures related to production processes, but not operating practices (columns 3 and 4). Consistent with our prediction, these results suggest that nearby SRMFs influence the treated firms to respond to a nearby enforcement action by reducing emissions at local treated plants in the short term and then gradually implementing abatement measures to reduce emissions at other distant plants in the long term.

5.3 Long-run toxic releases at local nontarget plants

To the extent that local regulatory risk is transitory (e.g., the EPA’s focus may shift to another industry or another area as a result of various factors, such as media attention and political influence), we conjecture that a parent firm could increase emissions at treated plants again in the long run after the EPA becomes less active in the area. At the same time, if local SRMFs hold genuine sustainability motives, their presence in the area could play a continuing monitoring role even when regulatory risk arising from a possible EPA spillover investigation subsides. Thus, we predict that the reversals of emissions in the long run could be prevented when a local SRMF is located in close proximity.

To examine the above predictions, we construct cohorts of treated and control plants using plant-year observations for the 3 years before and the [4, 10] years after each event year. We remove from the sample observations of the first 3 years after each event as well as events where there are confounding enforcement actions during the [4, 10] period. We further require control plants not to have been treated in a past event and not to become treated in the next 10 years. We then estimate the following plant-cohort-year level regression:

5.4 Firm-level evidence

Our plant-level results show that treated firms reduce toxic emissions at local treated plants. We find that firms that respond to a nearby enforcement action by reducing emissions experience lowerROAin the next 3 years, and the negative impact is larger if an SRMF is located near the local plant. InInternetAppendixTable IA12, we find similar results for firms that invest in process improvements, but no such results for firms that implement changes in operating practices. We conjecture that the short-term impact on bottom lines is confined to firms that engage in more stringent waste management methods and more extensive operational changes that increase operational costs. We further find that, during the [4, 10] years after enforcement actions, these treated firms that have reduced toxic releases and implemented process-related abatement measures experience improvements inROA. It is beyond the scope of our analysis to evaluate whether these abatement measures are value-enhancing or whether the long-term improvements inROApersist.42,43

2.1 Plant-level toxic releases measures

We obtain a plant’s toxic emissions information from the EPA’s Toxics Release Inventory (TRI) program. The EPA requires all plants that meet their reporting criteria (e.g., carrying at least 10 employees, operating in certain industry sectors, and using certain type of chemicals) to report emissions data.15On the reporting forms, plants are required to provide the number of pounds per chemical that are released into the ground, air, and water. The TRI database also provides information on the plant’s identifier, each chemical’s identification information, quantities of each chemical released on-site at the plant each year, and quantities of each chemical transferred off-site to other locations each year.

To identify whether a chemical in the TRI is harmful to humans, we obtain information on the toxicity of chemicals from the EPA’s Integrated Risk Information System (IRIS), which assesses, characterizes, and standardizes the health hazards of over 400 chemicals in the environment.16Using the common chemical identifier—Chemical Abstract Services (CAS) numbers—we match IRIS chemicals with TRI data to identify chemicals that can harm human health. Our plant-level toxic emissions measure,Toxic, is then a plant’s total quantity of on-site releases of all identified harmful chemicals. To ensure that toxic releases are comparable across plants, we standardize toxic releases using the industry mean and standard deviation in each year. We confirm that our results do not change qualitatively when we do not standardize toxic releases. In addition, we employ the Risk-Screening Environmental Indicators (RSEI) score, provided by the EPA, as an alternative measure of a plant’s toxic releases.RSEIis an aggregate weighted measure of chemical releases, incorporating the chemical’s fate and transport through the environment, each chemical’s relative toxicity, and potential for human exposure, among other factors.ln(RSEI)is the natural logarithm of one plus a plant’sRSEIscore.

Plants are mandated to disclose their toxic releases, meaning the TRI data could be subject to self-report bias. Recent research, however, suggests that such bias is idiosyncratic and on average likely to be negligible (Brehm and Hamilton 1996;EPA 1998;De Marchi and Hamilton 2006;Akey and Appel 2021). Moreover, truthful reports of high emissions are not automatically penalized (Greenstone 2003). Indeed, the EPA’s Incentives for Self-Policing: Discovery, Disclosure, Correction and Prevention of Violations policy is designed to encourage plants to disclose violations voluntarily, allowing violators to avoid up to 100%of severity-based penalties and criminal prosecution if they are cooperative and their disclosure meets the conditions specified under the policy.17In contrast, plants that misreport their emissions can face civil penalties or criminal charges (Greenstone 2003;Xu and Kim 2022).

Although high emission quantities are not automatically penalized, this does not necessarily imply that plants do not have to reduce emissions.Blundell, Gowrisankaran, and Langer (2020)document that, once the EPA uncovers a violation, it will place the violating plant under “violator” status, which subjects the plant to additional inspections, potentially uncovering additional violations and generating additional fines. Plants will return to compliance status only once those violations have been resolved. Thus, the cost to the plant of being a violator comes not only from the investment costs required to resolve outstanding violations but also from stricter regulatory oversight. Given these costs, plants have strong incentives to reduce toxic releases if they wish to avoid being labeled as violators. Moreover, SRMFs that are located in the area near a violating plant can also exert pressure on investees’ nearby plants to reduce toxic releases.

2.2 Plant-level data

We obtain information on plants operated by U.S. public firms between 1990 and 2015 from the National Establishment Time-Series (NETS) database, which is supplied by Dun and Bradstreet (D{&}B) and is maintained by Walls and Associates. An important feature of the NETS database is that it does not suffer from survivorship bias (Addoum, Ng, and Ortiz-Bobea 2020). Each plant is given a unique identifier (DUNS number), which does not change even when the plant changes its location. This thereby allows us to trace a plant’s locations throughout its entire life. We obtain the plant’s historical location information (FIPS code, state, county, and longitude and latitude coordinates) and its parent company names from NETS. We then match parent companies in the NETS database with firms in Compustat and the Center for Research in Security Prices (CRSP) by their historical legal names, supplemented with careful manual checking. We merge plant-level toxic emissions data from the TRI with historical plant locations from NETS using a TRI-linking table obtained from the EPA, which provides links between DUNS numbers and TRI plant identifiers. We manually match TRI plants that do not have DUNS numbers with plants in NETS using plant identifying information, such as location, name, and the parent firm’s name.

Neumark, Wall, and Zhang (2011)find that the correlations between NETS and Current Employment Statistics (CES) and Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages (QCEW) are 0.99 and 0.95 at the county-by-industry level, respectively.Barrot and Nanda (2020)note that business entities are required to register with Dun {&} Bradstreet (which supplies the source data to NETS) and obtain a DUNS number if they wish to bid on government contracts. On the other hand, while the NETS data are fairly reliable for cross-sectional analyses, they have been found to be problematic for tracking establishment- and firm-level outcomes over time and thus for analyzing business dynamism (see, e.g.,Crane and Decker 2020).

2.3 EPA enforcement actions data

We obtain data on all enforcement actions from the EPA’s Integrated Compliance Information System (ICIS), which contains civil, judicial, and administrative federal EPA enforcement cases. The ICIS provides historical records for all enforcement actions taken by the EPA under the following environmental statutes: the Clean Air Act (CAA), the Clean Water Act (CWA), the Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA), the Emergency Planning and Community Right-to-Know Act (EPCRA) Section 313, the Toxic Substances Control Act (TSCA), the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA), the Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act (CERCLA or Superfund), the Safe Drinking Water Act (SDWA), and the Marine Protection, Research, and Sanctuaries Act (MPRSA).

We collect all formal EPA enforcement cases that result in a conclusion (i.e., a final decision). For each enforcement case, we obtain information on the case-unique identifier, milestone dates (from initiation to conclusion), plants charged in the case, and the magnitudes of the penalties (i.e., state/local penalties, the total compliance costs, and federal cost recovery totals). We match these enforcement cases with NETS/TRI plants using the TRI-linking table provided by the EPA. We identify a plant as violator if the EPA takes an enforcement action against it in a given year.

2.4 Text-Based Network Industry Classifications and peer firms

To identify a firm’s competing peers in the same product market, we employHoberg and Phillips’s (2016) Text-based Network Industry Classifications (TNIC), which are based on firm-level pairwise similarity scores from a textual analysis of product descriptions in 10-K annual filings. Since our hypothesis concerns peer firms that operate in the same product market, TNIC is more suitable for our empirical analysis because it is time-varying and is updated as firms change their products. We use two types of classification: TNIC3, which is calibrated to approximate three-digit SIC codes, and TNIC2, which is constructed to match the coarseness level of two-digit SIC classifications (Hoberg and Phillips 2010,2010). Only firm pairs competing in the same TNIC3 product market are identified as competing peers, while firm pairs identified as competing in the same TNIC2 but different TNIC3 product markets are deemed nonpeers.

2.5 Sample and summary statistics

After combining TRI data, NETS data, enforcement actions data, and TNIC data for each plant, we identify its nearby TNIC3-peer plants that are located within a radius of 100 miles.18In a given year, we identify a plant as a treated plant if the EPA takes an enforcement action against any of its nearby (i.e., located within 100 miles) peer plants during the year. Since a treated plant’s own environmental violation can confound the effect of its peer’s violation, we remove plant-year observations if, in the event year and the 3 years following the enforcement action, the EPA takes an enforcement action against the treated plant itself. To construct a control group of plants whose products are reasonably related to those of the treated plant and the violating peer plant, we drop plants operated by firms that do not compete in the same TNIC2 product market. We also require plants to have toxic release information and location data available as well as to have nonmissing firm-level control variables.

After constructing cohorts of treated and control plants for the stacked difference-in-differences (DiD) framework, we obtain our final sample, which contains 158,211 plant-year observations between 1990 and 2015. An average firm in our sample has a market capitalization of $2.54 billion, aBook-to-marketratio of 0.49, aReturn on assetsratio of 0.14, sales of $3.23 billion, and aLeverageratio of 0.20.

3. Spillover Effects of EPA Enforcement Actions

Our analysis focuses on nontarget plants, defined as those that in a given year had not experienced an EPA enforcement action within the preceding 3 years or within the following 3 years. The first hypothesis we examine in this study is that, in the presence of compliance slack, nontarget plants respond to an EPA enforcement action against a nearby TNIC3-peer plant by reducing harmful toxic releases. To motivate the use of the Hoberg and Phillips TNIC3 classification, inInternetAppendixTable IA1, we examine whether plant pairs competing in the same TNIC3 product market release similar chemicals that are harmful to human health. Using a plant-pair-level measure of correlations of toxic releases between TNIC plant-pairs and a firm-pair-level measure of technological proximity between TNIC firm-pairs, we find that these correlations are stronger among TNIC3 peers than among those in the same TNIC2 industry (but not the same TNIC3 industry). These results suggest that TNIC3 reasonably reflects the similarity in harmful toxic releases as well as the similarity in technology between peer firms.

3.1 Nontarget plants’ responses to nearby enforcement actions

Having shown that peer plants in the same TNIC3 tend to release similar harmful chemicals (compared to nonpeer plants), we now examine whether nontarget plants reduce their toxic releases after the EPA takes an enforcement action against a peer plant located in close proximity. We use a stacked difference-in-differences (DiD) framework to compare changes in toxic releases by treated plants and control plants around EPA enforcement actions. It is useful to note that our stacked DiD approach differs from the traditional generalized DiD method. In our setting, where there are multiple periods and plants are treated at different points in time, estimation bias may arise with the generalized DiD method as the control group contains previously treated plants and treated plants may have already been treated (Goodman-Bacon 2021;Sun and Abraham 2021;Roth et al. 2022). To address these issues with the traditional DiD approach, we follow recent studies and use a stacked DiD method which removes all control plants that have been treated (Gormley and Matsa 2011,2011;Goodman-Bacon 2021;Baker, Larcker, and Wang 2022). Nevertheless, we also confirm that all of our results continue to hold when we use the generalized DiD approach, although the economic magnitudes of the estimated treatment effects are larger in the generalized DiD setting.

FollowingGormley and Matsa (2011), for each year in which enforcement actions occur, we construct a sample (cohort) of treated plants and control plants using plant-year observations for the 3 years before and the 3 years after an enforcement event, removing the event year. Treated plants are those that are not targeted by the EPA but are located within 100-mile radius of violating TNIC3-peer plants.19Control plants include (a) plants operated by the same parent firm as a treated plant but located outside the 100-miles radius of the violating peer plant, (b) plants operated by other TNIC3-peer firms located outside the 100-mile radius of the violating peer plant, and (c) plants operated by firms that compete outside the TNIC3 product market but in the same TNIC2 market as treated plants.20We remove plants run by firms that are outside the TNIC2 network of a treated plant’s parent firm. To avoid any contamination of our control group, we require that control plants in each cohort not have been treated in a past cohort and will not be treated in the 6 years following an enforcement event (Baker, Larcker, and Wang 2022). This procedure results in a total of 20 cohorts over our sample period, each representing a year in which enforcement actions occur.21

Table 1, panel B, reports the number of treated plants and the number of control plants in each event year (cohort). On average, a cohort contains 313 treated plants and 2,206 control plants. We stack these cohorts across all years and estimate the following plant-cohort-year panel regression:
